🇮🇪 SciFest Ireland 2026

Is Ozempic the Solution to Obesity in Ireland?

A public health analysis examining GLP-1 inhibitors, gastric bypass surgery, and the complex reality of treating Ireland's obesity crisis.

👩‍🔬 Pari Kohli
👩‍🔬 Leah Henson
Scroll to explore ↓
The Problem

Obesity in Ireland: A Growing Crisis

Ireland has the second highest rates of obesity in Europe. Despite this, obesity remains heavily stigmatised — a chronic disease that is too often blamed on the patient rather than treated as the complex medical condition it truly is.

Obesity is defined by the World Health Organisation as "abnormal or excessive fat accumulation that presents a risk to health." BMI is the most widely used clinical measurement, calculated by dividing weight in kg by height in metres squared.

The strain on Ireland's HSE is significant. Obesity contributes hundreds of millions in annual direct healthcare costs and lost productivity. Higher obesity rates drive increased cases of type 2 diabetes, heart disease, certain cancers, and musculoskeletal issues — requiring more complex and prolonged treatment. The volume of people needing weight management strains primary care, acute services, and specialist services alike. Managing obese patients in hospitals can also increase risks of complications like pneumonia or pressure ulcers, delaying care and pushing costs even higher.

The three main treatment approaches in Ireland are weight management programmes, GLP-1 inhibitors, and gastric bypass surgery.

2nd
Highest obesity rates in Europe
€100m+
Estimated annual HSE costs from obesity
3
Main treatment approaches in Ireland

Students surveyed in our school

EU Obesity Prevalence — Ireland ranks 2nd highest

Understanding Root Causes

Why Do People Become Obese?

Most people attribute obesity to diet and inactivity alone — but the science tells a more nuanced story. Multiple intersecting factors contribute to the disease.

🧬
Genetics
Inherited factors influence metabolism, appetite regulation, and how the body stores fat.
🧠
Psychological Factors
Stress, emotional eating, and mental health conditions directly influence eating patterns.
🏙️
Obesogenic Environment
Constant availability of cheap, unhealthy food makes healthy choices structurally harder.
🍔
Dietary Habits
High-calorie, low-nutrition diets combined with irregular eating patterns contribute significantly.
💊
Medical Conditions
Conditions like hypothyroidism, or certain medications, can cause weight gain independent of lifestyle.
🪑
Physical Inactivity
Sedentary lifestyles, desk work, and reduced movement contribute to energy imbalance.

The Medication

GLP-1 Inhibitors
Explained

The Medication

What Are GLP-1 Inhibitors?

GLP-1 (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1) receptor agonists are medications that mimic a natural hormone your body produces in the gut. They help regulate blood sugar, slow digestion, and signal fullness — making them effective for both type 2 diabetes and weight management.

How GLP-1 Works in Your Body

1
Stimulates insulin release when blood sugar rises
2
Slows gastric emptying — food moves more slowly from stomach to intestine
3
Signals the brain that you are full, reducing appetite
4
Decreases liver sugar production, improving blood glucose control
Defintion

GLP-1 stands for glucagon-like peptide-1, a hormone your body naturally produces in the gut. Its job is to help regulate blood sugar by increasing insulin secretion, slowing down how quickly your stomach empties, and signalling your brain that you're full. GLP-1 receptor agonists are medications that mimic this hormone's effects, amplifying those natural processes.

Benefits

Their benefits also extend to heart and kidney health. Common examples include Ozempic/Wegovy (semaglutide) and Mounjaro (tirzepatide, which also targets GIP).

How GLP-1 Inhibitors Make People Lose Weight

GLP-1 receptor agonists mimic the natural hormone of GLP-1. They help with obesity because they delay gastric emptying, which means food moves more slowly from your stomach to your intestines. This means a person does not get as hungry as they would normally when not on medication.

Article Reference

An article we read to find out how Ozempic works:

wmchealth.org — Beyond the Ozempic Buzz: How GLP-1s Actually Work

We also read an important article from UCD about a clinical trial in Ireland:

ucd.ie — Clinical Trial in Ireland Challenges Beliefs About Ozempic and Similar New Obesity Treatments

The article explains that a clinical trial in Ireland challenged the common belief that drugs like Ozempic are a complete solution to obesity. The study, carried out at St Vincent's University Hospital, found that while these medications can help people lose weight, their effects depend strongly on lifestyle factors such as diet, physical activity, and ongoing medical support. Researchers emphasised that obesity is a complex condition and that medications should not replace long-term lifestyle and behavioural changes. Instead, the findings suggest that treatments like Ozempic are most effective when used as part of a wider, personalised approach rather than as a quick or standalone fix.

Medication

They have the same name but different brand names and are advertised as separate things. Weight loss would have a higher dose.

Generic name Brand name Approved use
Semaglutide injection Ozempic Type 2 diabetes
Semaglutide injection Wegovy Weight loss
Liraglutide Victoza Type 2 diabetes
Liraglutide Saxenda Weight loss

Common GLP-1 Medications

Ozempic / Wegovy Semaglutide — Diabetes / Weight Loss
Rybelsus Oral Semaglutide — Diabetes
Victoza / Saxenda Liraglutide — Diabetes / Weight Loss
Trulicity Dulaglutide — Weekly injection
Mounjaro / Zepbound Tirzepatide — Dual GIP/GLP-1

⚠️ Important Side Effects

Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, constipation, muscle mass loss ("Ozempic face"), and weight regain upon discontinuation. Using Ozempic for weight loss has also caused shortages for diabetes patients who depend on it.

Our Key Insight

The Training Wheels Approach

We believe Ozempic is not the full solution — but a part of one. Just as training wheels help a child learn to ride a bike, Ozempic should support the patient while they build permanent healthy habits. This concept is also used with antidepressant medication — the support is temporary, and it helps people engage in lasting lifestyle changes.

While on Ozempic, the patient must also maintain habits such as a healthy diet and regular exercise. This way, the patient can slowly get off Ozempic without regaining all the weight back — which is what typically happens with Ozempic alone. Ozempic must never replace a healthy lifestyle; it must be a bridge to one.

🚴
Start Ozempic
With diet & exercise support
💪
Build Habits
Healthy lifestyle becomes routine
✅
Taper Off
Maintain weight independently

Obesity is a chronic disease and should be treated as such — not just by professionals, but by the public too. We must stop blaming patients and understand the many complex factors that lead to the disease.

— Pari Kohli & Leah Henson, SciFest 2026
Treatment Options

Gastric Bypass vs. Ozempic

Both are legitimate, evidence-based treatments. The right choice depends on the individual patient's circumstances, preferences, and medical history.

Factor 💉 Ozempic (Semaglutide) 🔪 Gastric Bypass Surgery
Expected Weight Loss 10–15% body weight over 30–56 weeks 25–35% total body weight in 12–24 months
Duration Weekly injections — must continue for lasting effect Permanent surgical change
Weight Regain High risk if discontinued Possible without lifestyle changes
Mortality Risk 94 reported deaths (still young data) 1 in every 1,250 patients
Side Effects Nausea, muscle loss, "Ozempic face" Dumping syndrome, food intolerance, acid reflux
Diabetes Impact Strong blood sugar control Strong — often resolves type 2 diabetes
Accessibility Injectable medication — shortage concerns for diabetics Requires surgery eligibility, anaesthesia risk
Key Benefits Reduces cravings, controls blood sugar, no surgery required Reduces stomach size, alters hunger hormones (GLP-1), sustained long-term loss; often resolves type 2 diabetes, high BP, high cholesterol & sleep apnoea
Best used as "training wheels" with lifestyle support Best for severe obesity where other methods failed
Primary Research

What Does the Public Think?

We surveyed 38 TY students in our school to understand public perceptions of Ozempic and obesity treatment.

Survey Response: "What is Ozempic?" — Word Cloud

What do you think Ozempic is used for?
🔵 Weight Loss   🟢 Diabetes

92% associated Ozempic with weight loss — not its primary purpose

Should Ozempic be used for weight loss?
🔵 Yes (18%)   🩷 No (82%)

82% of students said no to using Ozempic for weight loss

Who have you heard of using Ozempic?
🔵 None   🩷 Celebrities   🟢 Someone you know

66% heard of Ozempic through celebrities

Best weight loss method for obesity?
🔵 Exercise (53%)   🩷 Surgery (26%)   🟢 Medication (21%)

Exercise was the top answer — 0% said medication

Do you think Ozempic should be used for weight loss?
No
82%

18%
Who have you heard of using Ozempic?
Celebrities
~55%
None / Unknown
~30%
Someone you know
~15%

💡 What the Survey Tells Us

The majority of people are not fully aware of what Ozempic really is. Most people associate medication with "taking the easy way out." This reveals a significant gap in public awareness — obesity IS a chronic disease, and medication is a medically recognised treatment. More education and open conversation about obesity is needed in Ireland.

Clinical Evidence

What the Research Shows

A clinical trial at St Vincent's University Hospital, Ireland, challenged the common belief that drugs like Ozempic are a complete solution to obesity.

The study found that while these medications help people lose weight, their effects depend strongly on lifestyle factors — diet, physical activity, and ongoing medical support. Researchers emphasised that obesity is a complex condition and that medications should not replace long-term lifestyle and behavioural changes.

The findings suggest that treatments like Ozempic are most effective when used as part of a wider, personalised approach rather than as a quick or standalone fix.

🏥
Lifestyle Dependent
Ozempic's effectiveness is strongly linked to diet, physical activity, and ongoing medical support.
🔬
Not a Standalone Fix
Medication alone does not address the root causes of obesity — behavioural changes are essential.
🤝
Personalised Approach
A wider, personalised care plan combining medication, diet, and support yields the best outcomes.

Obesity is a chronic disease like type 2 diabetes or hypertension — we treat those with medication alongside lifestyle support. We should do the same for obesity, without blaming the patient.

— Pari Kohli & Leah Henson, based on clinical evidence review, SciFest 2026
Our Findings

So, Is Ozempic the Solution?

After extensive research, a survey, and clinical evidence review, our conclusion is clear — Ozempic is part of the solution, not a standalone cure.

Our Conclusion

Ozempic should be used as a last resort — only when diet and exercise have not been effective. It must be used alongside healthy lifestyle habits so that patients can eventually taper off without regaining weight. It is not a replacement for a healthy lifestyle; it is a bridge to one.

Changing the Stigma

Obesity is a chronic disease influenced by genetics, psychology, environment, and hormones. Blaming patients is both unfair and counter-productive. Medical treatment — whether medication or surgery — should be destigmatised and understood as valid healthcare.

Exercise & Diet First

We firmly believe exercise and diet should always be the first course of action. Medication and surgery should only be considered when lifestyle changes alone are not sufficient. Knowing a medical solution exists can actually motivate people, not discourage them.

The Bigger Picture

A clinical trial at St Vincent's University Hospital confirmed that GLP-1 medications are most effective as part of a wider, personalised approach — not as a quick fix. Ireland needs a comprehensive, compassionate obesity strategy.

🏆 SciFest Ireland 2026 — Public Health Category
